stage.name: Checkpoint 01: do you know where to start reading?
stage.description: A choice-only orientation checkpoint covering CVA6 positioning, IP boundaries, source routing, and first architecture map readiness.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: A real checkpoint before deeper CVA6 reading

content.title: Checkpoint 01: do you know where to start reading?
content.subtitle: Before configuration and pipeline lessons, verify that your map is stable: identity, boundary, source role, and first reading route.
content.thesis: If you cannot route the question, you are not ready to trust the answer.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README repository positioning
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md project overview and directory structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README orients readers to the CVA6 repository, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and the difference between the core and the APU material.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual Scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual lists the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF as in-scope, and CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support as out of scope.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document describes a specific configuration and provides information for RTL modification and RTL verification rather than software-only instruction lookup.

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint is deliberately practical. It is not asking you to memorize every acronym in CVA6. It is asking whether you can start in the right place. Can you describe what CVA6 is, avoid calling it a full SoC, choose the right official document, and keep optional features scoped? If yes, the next unit will be much easier.

actions.text: This checkpoint is deliberately practical. It is not asking you to memorize every acronym in CVA6. It is asking whether you can start in the right place. Can you describe what CVA6 is, avoid calling it a full SoC, choose the right official document, and keep optional features scoped? If yes, the next unit will be much easier.

scenes.title: The four skills Unit 01 should leave behind

content.title: Four things should now feel automatic
content.subtitle: These are small habits, but they decide whether later technical depth will stay clean.

items.title: Identity
items.body: CVA6 is a configurable, application-class RISC V processor core family.

items.title: Boundary
items.body: Core IP, optional units, interfaces, and external SoC objects should not be mixed.

items.title: Source role
items.body: README, User Manual, Requirements, and Design Docs answer different kinds of questions.

items.title: Reading route
items.body: Architecture reading begins from a map, not from a random module filename.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 project family goal
sourceAnchors.locator: overview/index.md CVA6 application class RISC-V CPU core
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview states that CVA6 is a family of production-quality, open-source, application-class RISC-V CPU cores targeting ASIC and FPGA implementations.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual Scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual lists the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF as in-scope, and CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support as out of scope.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation organization
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview separates the User Guide, Requirements Specification, Design Document, configuration-specific design documents, and APU documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document describes a specific configuration and provides information for RTL modification and RTL verification rather than software-only instruction lookup.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 01 leaves you with four habits. First, identity: CVA6 is a configurable application-class RISC V processor core family. Second, boundary: core IP and external SoC services are different layers. Third, source role: the README, User Manual, Requirements, and design documents each have a job. Fourth, reading route: architecture learning starts with a map.

actions.text: Unit 01 leaves you with four habits. First, identity: CVA6 is a configurable application-class RISC V processor core family. Second, boundary: core IP and external SoC services are different layers. Third, source role: the README, User Manual, Requirements, and design documents each have a job. Fourth, reading route: architecture learning starts with a map.

scenes.title: Question routing is the real skill

content.title: Choose the source before giving the answer
content.subtitle: The same keyword can require different sources depending on the question being asked.

cards.title: Repository mechanics
cards.body: Use README when the question is build entry, folder ownership, or setup flow.

cards.title: Scope and family
cards.body: Use User Manual when the question is what CVA6 is, what it includes, or which configurations exist.

cards.title: Shall-level behavior
cards.body: Use Requirements when the question is contract, optional feature, or verification scope.

cards.title: How a variant works
cards.body: Use the matching Design Document when the question is module behavior in a concrete configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 GitHub README repository positioning
sourceAnchors.locator: github-openhwgroup-cva6/README.md project overview and directory structure
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README orients readers to the CVA6 repository, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and the difference between the core and the APU material.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation organization
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview separates the User Guide, Requirements Specification, Design Document, configuration-specific design documents, and APU documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual Scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual lists the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF as in-scope, and CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support as out of scope.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document describes a specific configuration and provides information for RTL modification and RTL verification rather than software-only instruction lookup.

content.speakerNotes: The most important habit is question routing. The same keyword can mislead you. Cache can be a scope question, a configuration question, or a module implementation question. Those are not the same. So before answering, identify the question type. That is how you keep a micro-course concise without becoming vague.

actions.text: The most important habit is question routing. The same keyword can mislead you. Cache can be a scope question, a configuration question, or a module implementation question. Those are not the same. So before answering, identify the question type. That is how you keep a micro-course concise without becoming vague.

content.title: Make every sentence earn its confidence
content.subtitle: A professional course sounds specific because it knows the limits of its sources.

columns.title: Safe
columns.body: The User Manual places this item in scope; this named configuration sets this parameter; this design doc explains this module.

columns.title: Risky
columns.body: CVA6 always has this feature; every configuration behaves this way; the diagram proves implementation detail it does not actually show.

content.takeaway: The safest technical style is not weaker. It is more reusable.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual Scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual lists the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF as in-scope, and CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support as out of scope.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 optional feature parameter rule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification states that optional features are controlled by SystemVerilog parameters and are absent from the synthesized netlist when not selected.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document describes a specific configuration and provides information for RTL modification and RTL verification rather than software-only instruction lookup.

content.speakerNotes: Here is a useful distinction. A safe statement names the source and the layer: the User Manual places this in scope, this configuration sets that parameter, or this design document explains that module. A risky statement generalizes too early: CVA6 always has this, every variant behaves that way, or the diagram proves an implementation detail it does not show.

actions.text: Here is a useful distinction. A safe statement names the source and the layer: the User Manual places this in scope, this configuration sets that parameter, or this design document explains that module. A risky statement generalizes too early: CVA6 always has this, every variant behaves that way, or the diagram proves an implementation detail it does not show.

content.title: From orientation to configuration literacy
content.subtitle: The next unit turns boundary discipline into variant-aware reading.

steps.title: Name the family
steps.body: Separate CVA6 as a configurable repository family from named products.

steps.title: Choose a variant
steps.body: Use CV32A60X as a stable first learning target.

steps.title: Read the parameters
steps.body: Check XLEN, privilege, MMU, CV-X-IF, cache, and pipeline-related values.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.body: Make claims only for the configuration you actually checked.

content.takeaway: Unit 02 is where CVA6 stops being one noun and becomes a family of engineering choices.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 configuration family table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual table compares CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU by target, privilege levels, ISA, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 initial release configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements table identifies CV32A60X as an ASIC IMC 32-bit configuration with no FPU, CV-X-IF support, no MMU, and small L1 I/D caches.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X parameter configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X parameter table records values such as XLEN 32, VLEN 32, RVC true, MmuPresent false, CvxifEn true, PipelineOnly true, and single commit port behavior.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 02 is the natural next step. We will stop saying CVA6 as if it were one frozen thing, and start reading it as a configurable family. We will choose CV32A60X as the first learning target, inspect its profile, and learn which parameters change conclusions. This is the bridge from overview to real engineering reading.

actions.text: Unit 02 is the natural next step. We will stop saying CVA6 as if it were one frozen thing, and start reading it as a configurable family. We will choose CV32A60X as the first learning target, inspect its profile, and learn which parameters change conclusions. This is the bridge from overview to real engineering reading.

scenes.title: Checkpoint: are you ready for Unit 02?

questions.question: Which description best matches CVA6 at the start of this learning path?

options.label: A configurable application-class RISC V processor core family

questions.analysis: The project materials frame CVA6 as a configurable application-class RISC V processor core family.

questions.question: A learner asks where the repository keeps core source versus APU-related material. Which source is the first stop?
questions.analysis: Repository mechanics and top-level structure belong first to the README.

questions.question: Which source is best for a module-level explanation of CV32A60X behavior?
questions.analysis: Variant-specific module behavior needs the matching design document.

questions.question: Which habits belong to Unit 01 source discipline?

options.label: Check whether the item is in scope
Route the question to the right official source

questions.analysis: A, B, and D are core habits. Optional features must not be assumed.

questions.question: If a feature is optional, what is the next professional question?

options.label: Which configuration or parameter selects it?
Is it automatically enabled by the logo?
Can a quiz grade it with free text?

questions.analysis: Optional feature claims require configuration or parameter evidence.

questions.question: Why does the architecture map lesson come before deep pipeline modules?

options.label: It gives a route through the material before details compete for attention

questions.analysis: A first architecture map helps learners enter detail in a controlled order.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The README orients readers to the CVA6 repository, quick setup, top-level directory roles, and the difference between the core and the APU material.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual lists the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF as in-scope, and CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support as out of scope.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview separates the User Guide, Requirements Specification, Design Document, configuration-specific design documents, and APU documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint is the door to the next unit. Answer slowly. The point is not to guess the phrase I prefer; it is to decide which layer the question belongs to. Once you can do that, you can read official CVA6 material with much less noise and much more confidence.

actions.text: This checkpoint is the door to the next unit. Answer slowly. The point is not to guess the phrase I prefer; it is to decide which layer the question belongs to. Once you can do that, you can read official CVA6 material with much less noise and much more confidence.

content.title: You now have the map needed for configuration literacy
content.subtitle: The next lessons can be more technical because the reading discipline is already established.
takeaways: CVA6 identity is separate from SoC integration.
Scope diagrams limit claims as much as they enable claims.
content.nextStep: Begin Unit 02 with CVA6 family and variant naming, then lock onto CV32A60X as the first concrete learning target.
content.closing: A good checkpoint should feel slightly strict. That strictness is what lets later lessons go deeper.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 project family goal
sourceAnchors.locator: overview/index.md CVA6 application class RISC-V CPU core
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documentation overview states that CVA6 is a family of production-quality, open-source, application-class RISC-V CPU cores targeting ASIC and FPGA implementations.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual Scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual lists the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF as in-scope, and CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support as out of scope.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X Design Document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document describes a specific configuration and provides information for RTL modification and RTL verification rather than software-only instruction lookup.

content.speakerNotes: This closes the orientation unit. If this checkpoint feels slightly strict, good. Strictness here is what lets the next lessons become more technical without drifting. We can now talk about named configurations, parameters, and concrete profiles because the learner already knows how to ask what source, what boundary, and what exact configuration.
